original_abstract: Electrochemical oxidation of 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) at the anode offers a promising, energy-efficient route for simultaneous hydrogen production and the synthesis of value-added chemicals. While this process operates at significantly lower potentials (~0.1 V vs. RHE) compared to conventional water splitting, the underlying reaction mechanisms and the identity of key intermediates have remained elusive. In this study, we utilize in situ Raman and attenuated total reflectance infrared (ATR-IR) spectroscopy, combined with isotope labeling and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, to investigate HMF oxidation on Au-modified Ni foam. We identify two critical, pH-dependent gem-diol intermediates: the 5-(dihydroxymethyl)furan-2-methanol anion (DHMFM$^-$) and dianion (DHMFM$^{2-}$). Our findings reveal that the dianion intermediate facilitates H$_2$ generation via a hydride (H$^-$) transfer mechanism at low potentials. Conversely, the monoanion intermediate undergoes a proton (H$^+$) transfer to yield H$_2$O. We demonstrate that increasing the electrode potential accelerates the oxidation rate of DHMFM$^-$, which shifts the chemical equilibrium away from DHMFM$^{2-}$, thereby supressing and eventually terminating anodic hydrogen production. By resolving the competition between these two pathways and capturing the role of the gem-diol dianion, this work provides a fundamental framework for the rational design of electrocatalytic systems for biomass-derived aldehyde upgrading and hydrogen evolution.
humanized_abstract: The electrochemical oxidation of 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) at the anode is considered as a high-efficient method for biomass-derived aldehyde conversion, producing hydrogen and chemical products simultaneously. The HMF oxidation process occurs at a lower overpotential of ~0.1 V vs. RHE compared to water splitting. So, the reaction mechanism and important intermediates of this process are unclear. Here, the in situ Raman and attenuated total reflectance infrared (ATR-IR) spectroscopy, combined with isotope labeling and density functional theory (DFT) calculations are employed to investigate the oxidation process of HMF on Au-modified Ni foam electrode. Two pH-dependent gem-diol intermediates, namely the 5-(dihydroxymethyl)furan-2-methanol anion (DHMFM$^-$) and dianion (DHMFM$^{2-}$) are identified. The role of these two gem-diol intermediates in the H2 production process at low overpotential is unraveled. The DFT results confirm that the gem-diol dianion acts as a key intermediate, which facilitates the hydride (H$^-$) transfer for producing H2 at a lower overpotential, while the gem-diol monoanion undergoes proton (H$^+$) transfer to produce H2O. The oxidation rate of DHMFM$^-$ increases at a higher electrode potential, which shifts the reaction equilibrium to the side of DHMFM$^{2-}$, thus suppressing and eventually terminating the anodic hydrogen production. Our results clarify the competition of intermediates and identify the important role of the gem-diol dianion, which will guide the construction of efficient electrocatalysts for biomass-derived aldehyde upgrading and hydrogen production.

Two pH-dependent gem-diol intermediates, namely the 5-(dihydroxymethyl)furan-2-methanol anion (DHMFM$^-$) and dianion (DHMFM$^{2-}$) are identified. The role of these two gem-diol intermediates in the H2 production process at low overpotential is unraveled. The DFT results confirm that the gem-diol dianion acts as a key intermediate, which facilitates the hydride (H$^-$) transfer for producing H2 at a lower overpotential, while the gem-diol monoanion undergoes proton (H$^+$) transfer to produce H2O. Our results clarify the competition of intermediates and identify the important role of the gem-diol dianion, which will guide the construction of efficient electrocatalysts for biomass-derived aldehyde upgrading and hydrogen production.
document.input: Electrochemical oxidation of 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) at the anode offers a promising, energy-efficient route for simultaneous hydrogen production and the synthesis of value-added chemicals. While this process operates at significantly lower potentials (~0.1 V vs. RHE) compared to conventional water splitting, the underlying reaction mechanisms and the identity of key intermediates have remained elusive. In this study, we utilize in situ Raman and attenuated total reflectance infrared (ATR-IR) spectroscopy, combined with isotope labeling and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, to investigate HMF oxidation on Au-modified Ni foam. We identify two critical, pH-dependent gem-diol intermediates: the 5-(dihydroxymethyl)furan-2-methanol anion (DHMFM$^-$) and dianion (DHMFM$^{2-}$). Our findings reveal that the dianion intermediate facilitates H$_2$ generation via a hydride (H$^-$) transfer mechanism at low potentials. Conversely, the monoanion intermediate undergoes a proton (H$^+$) transfer to yield H$_2$O. We demonstrate that increasing the electrode potential accelerates the oxidation rate of DHMFM$^-$, which shifts the chemical equilibrium away from DHMFM$^{2-}$, thereby supressing and eventually terminating anodic hydrogen production. By resolving the competition between these two pathways and capturing the role of the gem-diol dianion, this work provides a fundamental framework for the rational design of electrocatalytic systems for biomass-derived aldehyde upgrading and hydrogen evolution.
